purpose: Candidate original scubaSeason discoveries. A candidate only belongs here when the claim is derived from internal cross-dataset analysis and an initial public-prior-art search did not find the same joined claim.

required: The claim must be reproducible from scubaSeason data joins, rankings, or comparisons.
The claim must not merely restate a known destination reputation, operator claim, or source dataset fact.
The claim must include an originality review with exact prior-art search queries.
The claim must use guarded language until reviewed by a human editor.

allowed: We did not find this exact joined claim in public search...

records.title: Jardines de la Reina has the densest threatened-species stack among above-baseline reefs in our atlas
records.claim: In the current scubaSeason data, Jardines de la Reina is the top location with coral cover above 35 percent and at least six threatened species signals in sightings/IUCN joins.
records.whyThisIsOurs: Public pages commonly describe Jardines as shark-rich, crocodile-rich, protected, and healthy. The candidate claim is narrower: it ranks the location across our joined reef-health, sightings, IUCN, fishing-pressure, and MPA fields.

logic: Filter IUCN category to VU, EN, or CR.
Aggregate threatened species count and recentRecordCount by location.
Join reef-health observed.coralCoverPercent and keep locations with cover >= 35.
Rank by threatened species richness, then total recentRecordCount.

queries: "Jardines de la Reina" "six" threatened species coral cover sharks crocodile goliath grouper
"Jardines de la Reina" "coral cover" "Caribbean reef shark" "American crocodile"
"Jardines de la Reina" "Goliath grouper" "Caribbean reef shark" "coral cover"
publicKnownContext: Jardines is publicly known as a protected Cuban dive destination with sharks and crocodiles.
The exact joined ranking against threatened species richness plus coral-cover threshold was not found in the initial search.

records.editorialCaveat: This is an atlas-relative claim, not a global ecological claim. The record counts are occurrence evidence, not per-dive probability.

records.title: Sogod Bay is a rare overlap of above-baseline reef cover and decade-scale whale shark identity evidence
records.claim: In our data, Sogod Bay is one of the clearest overlaps between an above-baseline reef-health signal and a long-running threatened megafauna identity record.
records.whyThisIsOurs: The reef-health and whale shark facts are public separately. The discovery candidate is the overlap: this location satisfies both a coral-cover comparison and a longitudinal endangered-species evidence threshold.

logic: Identify reef-health notes with explicit national or regional coral-cover baseline comparisons.
Join same location to threatened megafauna evidence from sightings, encounters, or notes.
Promote only when both the reef comparison and megafauna evidence have source-backed numeric signals.

computedSignals.megafaunaSignal: 350+ individual whale sharks documented over 10+ years

publicKnownContext: Whale shark sightings in Sogod Bay are public.
Panaon outer reef coral-cover claims are public.
The exact reef-health plus whale-shark identity overlap was not found in the initial search.

records.editorialCaveat: The strongest reef-cover number applies to outer Panaon Island, not the whole bay; render that sub-location caveat visibly.

records.title: Brothers Islands stack two critically endangered shark signals on moderate-high coral cover
records.claim: In our atlas data, Brothers Islands stands out as a Red Sea location where coral cover is above 40 percent and the top two threatened-species signals are both critically endangered sharks.
records.whyThisIsOurs: Oceanic whitetips and hammerheads at Brothers/Daedalus are known to divers. The candidate claim is the internal join between reef-health threshold, IUCN severity, and repeat sighting counts.

logic: Join sightings to IUCN and locations.
Filter locations with observed coral cover >= 40.
Rank by count of CR species signals and recentRecordCount.

queries: "Red Sea" "Brothers Islands" "three threatened species" diving coral cover
publicKnownContext: The shark encounters are known in dive tourism.
The exact coral-cover plus two-CR-species atlas ranking was not found in the initial search.

records.editorialCaveat: This is an internal atlas comparison. Do not imply Brothers is globally unique without a broader verified database.

records.title: Fakarava has the strongest threatened-shark repeat signal paired with low fishing pressure
records.claim: In the current sightings file, South Pass / Fakarava has the highest threatened-shark recentRecordCount, and the joined pressure record marks fishing pressure as low.
records.whyThisIsOurs: Fakarava's shark wall is famous. The original part is narrower: ranking threatened-shark repeat evidence across all atlas sightings and joining it to fishing-pressure context.

logic: Filter to shark species with IUCN category VU, EN, or CR.
Join pressure and reef-health context for the same location.

queries: "South Pass" Fakarava "grey reef shark" "coral cover" "fishing pressure"
publicKnownContext: Fakarava is publicly known for grey reef shark aggregations.
The exact atlas-ranked repeat-sighting plus low-fishing-pressure claim was not found in the initial search.

records.editorialCaveat: This should be rendered as 'strongest signal in our current sightings file,' not as the world's largest shark aggregation.
